[Travis] [Coveralls] [Docs] [PyPI]

# ErrorHandler

This is a handler for the python standard logging framework that can
be used to tell whether messages have been logged at or above a
certain level.

This can be useful when wanting to ensure that no errors have been
logged before committing data back to a database.

As an example, first, you set up the error handler:

```pycon
>>> from errorhandler import ErrorHandler
>>> e = ErrorHandler()
```

Then you can log and check the handler at any point to see if it has
been triggered:

```pycon
>>> e.fired
False
>>> from logging import getLogger
>>> logger = getLogger()
>>> logger.error('an error')
>>> e.fired
True
```

You can use the `fired` attribute to only perform actions when no
errors have been logged:

```pycon
>>> if e.fired:
...   print "Not updating files as errors have occurred"
Not updating files as errors have occurred
```

# Installation

Do the following in your virtualenv:

```
pip install errorhandler
```

# Documentation

The latest documentation can also be found at:
<http://errorhandler.readthedocs.org/en/latest/>

methods.name: errorhandler.tests.TestErrorHandler.test_level_greater_than_configured
documentation:
Messages logged at levels higher than that configured still
trigger the handler:

```pycon
>>> from logging import INFO
>>> e = ErrorHandler(INFO)
```

In this case, an ERROR will make a good example:

```pycon
>>> logger.error('an error')
>>> e.fired
True
```

```pycon
>>> e.remove()
```

methods.name: errorhandler.tests.TestErrorHandler.test_manual_install
documentation:
```pycon
>>> handlers = logging.getLogger().handlers[:]
```

You don't have to install when you create:

```pycon
>>> e = ErrorHandler(install=False)
```

```pycon
>>> handlers == logging.getLogger().handlers[:]
True
```

You install later by calling the `install` method:

```pycon
>>> e.install()
```

```pycon
>>> len(logging.getLogger().handlers[:])-len(handlers)
1
```

```pycon
>>> e.remove()
```

methods.name: errorhandler.tests.TestErrorHandler.test_install_twice
documentation:
```pycon
>>> handlers = logging.getLogger().handlers[:]
```

Installing twice isn't a problem:

```pycon
>>> e = ErrorHandler()
>>> len(logging.getLogger().handlers[:])-len(handlers)
1
```

```pycon
>>> e.install()
>>> len(logging.getLogger().handlers[:])-len(handlers)
1
```

```pycon
>>> e.remove()
```

methods.name: errorhandler.tests.TestErrorHandler.test_uninstall
documentation:
```pycon
>>> handlers = logging.getLogger().handlers[:]
```

```pycon
>>> e = ErrorHandler()
>>> len(logging.getLogger().handlers[:])-len(handlers)
1
```

Uninstalling removes the handler from the handlers list:

```pycon
>>> e.remove()
>>> len(logging.getLogger().handlers[:])-len(handlers)
0
>>> logging.getLogger().handlers[:]==handlers
True
```

methods.name: errorhandler.tests.TestErrorHandler.test_uninstall_twice
documentation:
```pycon
>>> handlers = logging.getLogger().handlers[:]
```

Uninstalling twice isn't a problem either:

```pycon
>>> e = ErrorHandler()
>>> len(logging.getLogger().handlers[:])-len(handlers)
1
```

```pycon
>>> e.remove()
>>> len(logging.getLogger().handlers[:])-len(handlers)
0
>>> logging.getLogger().handlers[:]==handlers
True
```

```pycon
>>> e.remove()
>>> len(logging.getLogger().handlers[:])-len(handlers)
0
>>> logging.getLogger().handlers[:]==handlers
True
```
